ryan dahl

[

Ryan Dahl

](https://www.google.com/search?q=Ryan+Dahl&kgmid=%2Fm%2F0hg_x1f&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwiOirv5wYOTAxVuEFkFHcDEO1sQ3egRegQIAhAC&gl=us)

 is an American software engineer and open-source advocate widely recognized for creating two of the most influential JavaScript runtimes: **Node.js** and **Deno**. ![Wikipedia](data:image/png;base64,)Wikipedia +1

Professional Highlights

-   **Node.js (2009):** Dahl revolutionized backend development by introducing Node.js, which allowed JavaScript to run on the server side using an asynchronous, non-blocking I/O model. He managed the project until 2012 before stepping away to focus on other research.
-   **Deno (2018):** After reflecting on several "design mistakes" in Node.js, Dahl introduced Deno. It is designed as a more secure, modern runtime with native TypeScript support and built-in tools like a linter and formatter.
-   **Current Role:** He is the co-founder and CEO of **Deno Land Inc.**, based in Brooklyn, New York. ![Wikipedia](data:image/png;base64,)Wikipedia +8

![Interview with Ryan Dahl, Creator of Node.js on his life and ...](data:image/jpeg;base64,)

![Ryan Dahl, and the story of his Wizardry in Opensource - DEV ...](data:image/jpeg;base64,)

![A RedMonk Conversation: Ryan Dahl on the Deno v Oracle ...](data:image/jpeg;base64,)

![Ryan Dahl, Node.js Creator, Wants to Rebuild How We ...](data:image/jpeg;base64,)

Recent Views & Advocacy (2025–2026)

-   **Future of Coding:** In early 2026, Dahl made headlines by stating that "**the era of humans writing code is over**". He believes AI will handle implementation, while developers will pivot to high-level system architecture and decision-making.
-   **JavaScript Trademark:** He has been a vocal advocate for freeing the "JavaScript" name, formally petitioning the USPTO to cancel Oracle's trademark to allow the community to use the term without legal ambiguity.
-   **JSR (JavaScript Registry):** Dahl recently introduced JSR as a modern, TypeScript-first alternative to npm, designed to simplify package publishing across different runtimes. ![Reddit](data:image/png;base64,)Reddit +7

Background

Born in 1981, Dahl originally studied **mathematics** at UC San Diego and the University of Rochester. He pivoted to software engineering after dropping out of his Ph.D. program to travel and work on web applications. ![Wikipedia](data:image/png;base64,)Wikipedia +3

Would you like to explore his specific **"10 Regrets About Node.js"** or more details on how **Deno 2.0** handles Node compatibility?

why deno

Deno was created by Ryan Dahl to fix "design regrets" from his first project, Node.js

. While Node.js revolutionized the industry, it was built before modern JavaScript standards (like ES Modules) and TypeScript existed, leading to a complex and often insecure ecosystem. ![DevsData](data:image/png;base64,)DevsData +3

Deno is often chosen for the following reasons:

1\. Security by Default

In Node.js, a script has full access to your file system and network by default. Deno is **sandboxed**; it requires explicit flags (e.g., `--allow-net`, `--allow-read`) to access any sensitive resources, preventing malicious packages from stealing data. ![Appwrite](data:image/png;base64,)Appwrite +3

2\. Native TypeScript & JSX Support

Deno runs **TypeScript and JSX out of the box** without needing external compilers like Babel or complex tsconfig.json setups. This eliminates "tooling fatigue" and allows you to start coding immediately. ![YouTube](data:image/png;base64,)YouTube +2

3\. All-in-One Toolchain ("Batteries Included") 

Instead of hunting for third-party libraries for basic tasks, Deno includes built-in tools: ![Medium](data:image/png;base64,)Medium +1

-   **Linter:** `deno lint`
-   **Formatter:** `deno fmt`
-   **Test Runner:** `deno test`
-   **Dependency Inspector:** `deno info`
-   **Compiler:** `deno compile` (turns your script into a single standalone executable). ![Medium](data:image/png;base64,)Medium +3

4\. Modern Web Standards

Deno prioritizes Web APIs like `fetch`, `WebSockets`, and `WebStorage`. Code written for Deno is often more compatible with browser environments, reducing the learning curve for frontend developers transitioning to the backend. ![Deno Docs](data:image/png;base64,)Deno Docs +3

5\. Simplified Dependency Management 

Deno originally replaced the massive `node_modules` folder with **URL-based imports** (e.g., `import { serve } from "https://deno.land..."`). However, with the release of **Deno 2.0**, it now offers full Node.js and npm compatibility, allowing you to use your favorite `npm` packages while still keeping Deno's security and speed. ![Meteor Forum](data:image/png;base64,)Meteor Forum +3

Summary Comparison

| Feature | Node.js | Deno |
| ------- | ------- | ---- |
| Security | Full access by default | Sandboxed (explicit permissions) |
| Language | JS (TS via transpilation) | JS & TS natively |
| Modules | CommonJS & ESM | ESM Only (native) |
| Packages | npm / package.json | URLs, JSR, and npm (Deno 2.0+) |
| Standard Lib | Minimal | Comprehensive & Audited |

Would you like to see a **code example** of a secure Deno server versus a traditional Node.js setup?

why node is still chosen most time

While Deno 2.0 and competitors like 

Bun have introduced modern improvements, **Node.js** remains the dominant choice in 2026 for several pragmatic reasons: 

1\. The "Package Power" (npm Ecosystem)

Node.js is built around npm, which hosts over **2.5 million packages**. ![Dev Genius](data:image/jpeg;base64,)Dev Genius +1

-   **Deep Integration:** Many mission-critical libraries (e.g., enterprise database drivers, specialized SDKs) are built specifically for Node's internal architecture.
-   **Reliability:** While Deno 2.0 has made huge strides in npm compatibility, edge cases still exist where certain "native" modules or older legacy packages may not work perfectly outside of Node. ![Medium](data:image/png;base64,)Medium +3

2\. Enterprise Stability & Hiring

For large-scale businesses, "boring" is a feature.

-   **Predictability:** Node.js has a decades-long track record of handling massive traffic for companies like Netflix and PayPal.
-   **Talent Pool:** It is significantly easier and cheaper to hire experienced Node.js developers. Most engineering teams already have the internal knowledge to debug, scale, and secure Node environments without retraining.
-   **LTS (Long-Term Support):** Node's formal LTS release schedule provides the multi-year stability guarantees that corporate IT departments require. ![Medium](data:image/png;base64,)Medium +4

3\. Tooling & Infrastructure Maturity

Node is deeply embedded in the modern development pipeline: 

-   **Cloud Support:** Every major cloud provider (AWS, Google Cloud, Azure) has "first-class" support for Node.js in their Serverless and PaaS offerings.
-   **Observability:** Industrial-grade monitoring tools like Datadog and New Relic have mature, battle-tested agents for Node.js that provide deep insights Deno is still catching up on. ![YouTube](data:image/png;base64,)YouTube +2

4\. High "Switching Costs"

For existing companies, the cost of migrating a massive Node.js codebase to Deno often outweighs the benefits. ![Xavor Corporation](data:image/png;base64,)Xavor Corporation +1

-   **Refactoring:** Even with compatibility layers, moving to a different security model and module system can require weeks of manual code audits.
-   **"If it ain't broke":** For most standard web APIs, Node's performance is already more than sufficient, making a move to a "faster" runtime a low priority. ![Medium](data:image/png;base64,)Medium +3

| Decision Factor | Why Choose Node.js | Why Choose Deno |
| --------------- | ------------------ | --------------- |
| Project Type | Large Enterprise / Legacy | Greenfield / Small Utility |
| Dependency Risk | High (needs rare npm libs) | Low (standards-focused) |
| Security Needs | Manual configuration | Sandboxed by default |
| Hiring | Massive talent pool | Emerging/Niche |

Would you like a list of the **specific npm packages** that still struggle to run on Deno, or a comparison of **startup times** for serverless functions?

i mean node could do almost everything deno could do

You are absolutely right. From a purely functional standpoint, there is virtually nothing Deno can do that Node.js cannot. If you want a secure, TypeScript-friendly, web-standard-compliant app in Node, you can get there—it just takes more "assembly."

The difference isn't about **capability**; it’s about **intent and ergonomics.** Here is how that "everything" compares when you actually have to build it:

1\. Security: "Opt-in" vs. "Opt-out"

-   **Node.js:** You *can* secure a Node app using tools like Docker or specialized permissions flags (introduced recently in Node 20+), but by default, every `npm install` has the keys to your house.
-   **Deno:** The security is "hard-coded" into the workflow. You don't have to remember to secure it; you have to intentionally "unsecure" it to make it work.

2\. TypeScript: "The Build Step" vs. "The Runtime"

To get TypeScript running in Node, you usually have to:

1.  Install `typescript` and `ts-node` (or `tsx`).
2.  Configure a `tsconfig.json`.
3.  Set up a build script to compile `.ts` to `.js`.
4.  Manage source maps so your error logs make sense.

In **Deno**, you just type `deno run main.ts`. It treats TypeScript as a first-class citizen, removing the friction of the "transpilation" step entirely.

3\. The "Configuration Tax"

Node.js is a "kit" while Deno is a "product." To get a modern Node project started, you often end up with a "black hole" of configuration files:

-   `.eslintrc` (Linting)
-   `.prettierrc` (Formatting)
-   `jest.config.js` (Testing)
-   `package.json` (Dependencies)
-   `tsconfig.json` (Types)

**Deno replaces all of those with the `deno` binary.** It’s the difference between buying a car where you have to install the radio, seats, and GPS yourself (Node) versus one that comes fully loaded from the factory (Deno).

4\. Convergence (The "Deno Effect")

Interestingly, because Deno exists, **Node.js is actually getting better.**  
Because of the competition from Deno and Bun, Node has recently added:

-   A built-in test runner (`node --test`).
-   Experimental permission flags (`--allow-fs-read`).
-   Native `fetch` support.
-   Experimental TypeScript support (Node 22.6+).

> **The Verdict:** You are right—Node *can* do everything. People choose Deno because they want those features to be **standardized and zero-effort**, rather than something they have to maintain themselves across every project.

If you were starting a **brand new, small project** today, would you prefer the "standard" reliability of Node, or are you looking for the "speed-to-code" of Deno?
